title: SEGMENT 9 — Read length distribution: 10-25 kb is the sweet spot

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — Read length distribution: 10-25 kb is the sweet spot
Let's talk about what a HiFi read actually looks like.
Concretely.
How long are these reads, in practice, and how do they distribute across a sequencing run?
A typical HiFi library is built from DNA fragments sheared to about fifteen to twenty kilobases.

After SMRTbell prep — ligating the hairpins — and size selection on a special bead-based system to enrich for the right size range, you end up with a population of SMRTbell molecules whose insert sizes are tightly distributed around the target.
You load that library onto a SMRT cell and run the instrument.
The polymerase makes many passes around each SMRTbell, generating the multi-pass polymerase read.
After the run, the CCS software takes those polymerase reads, identifies the passes by locating the hairpin sequences, aligns them, and outputs HiFi reads.
The HiFi read length is the length of one pass — which is the insert size of the SMRTbell.
In practice, a typical HiFi run produces reads distributed roughly between ten and twenty-five kilobases, with a peak around fifteen to twenty.
That's your sweet spot.
Long enough to span essentially all small repeats, all gene regions, almost all individual structural variants.
Short enough to get many passes per molecule, hence very high accuracy.
To give you a sense of why fifteen to twenty kilobases is so useful, let me compare it to what fits in a read.
A typical human gene — including its exons and introns — is somewhere between ten and a hundred kilobases long.
The median is maybe twenty kilobases.
So a single HiFi read can span an entire small-to-medium gene from start to finish, with no assembly required.
That's huge for things like phasing — figuring out which variants are on the same chromosome — because variants linked on a single read are by definition on the same parental chromosome.
A typical processed mRNA transcript is one to five kilobases.
A single HiFi read can easily span an entire transcript, including the 5' UTR, all the exons stitched together, and the 3' UTR with its polyA tail.
That's what makes PacBio Iso-Seq — full-length transcript sequencing — possible.
We'll get to that in segment fifteen.
A typical structural variant — an insertion, deletion, or inversion of, say, a few hundred to a few thousand bases — fits comfortably within a single HiFi read.
The breakpoints, the inserted sequence, the flanking context — all visible in one piece of data.
Compare this to Illumina, where structural variants have to be inferred from indirect signals (split reads, discordant pairs, abnormal coverage) because no single read can span the event.
HiFi makes structural variant calling direct and unambiguous.

A typical region of segmental duplication — where multi-kilobase chunks of the genome have been copied to multiple loci — is often spannable by a single HiFi read if the unique flanking sequence is captured.
This is what allows HiFi to resolve segmental duplications that short reads cannot.
PacBio has also experimented with longer inserts, in modes sometimes called "ultralong HiFi" or just "long-insert" runs.
With inserts of thirty to fifty kilobases, you can get longer reads, but you sacrifice some accuracy because the polymerase completes fewer passes per molecule.
With inserts of a hundred kilobases or more, you essentially fall back to CLR-style noisy reads with one or two passes — useful for some niche assembly problems but not the HiFi standard.
For comparison, Oxford Nanopore's reads are typically much longer — twenty to a hundred kilobases is routine, and with the right library prep you can get individual reads in the megabase range.
Nanopore reads are longer than PacBio HiFi reads, often by a factor of five or ten.
But they're noisier — typical Nanopore Q-scores for simplex reads are around Q15 to Q20, an order of magnitude worse than HiFi.
So the two platforms occupy slightly different niches.
Need maximum length, willing to tolerate some noise — Nanopore.
Need maximum accuracy at moderate length — PacBio HiFi.
We'll come back to this comparison in detail in segment sixteen.
One last point.
The read length distribution out of a HiFi run isn't uniform.
There's a peak, but there are also tails — some shorter reads from molecules where the polymerase quit early, some longer reads from rare oversized inserts that got through size selection.
When you're planning an experiment, you typically calculate your expected coverage in terms of bases sequenced, not reads sequenced.
A typical Revio run produces something like one hundred and twenty gigabases of HiFi data per SMRT cell.
Divided by an average read length of seventeen kilobases, that's roughly seven million reads per cell.
For a thirty-x coverage human genome (which has three gigabases), you need about ninety gigabases of HiFi data, which fits on a single Revio cell with room to spare.
That's a huge change from earlier generations — in 2019 it took a full Sequel II run to get thirty-x human coverage; now you can get it on a fraction of a Revio cell.
The economics, in other words, have shifted dramatically.
We'll cover that in segment eleven.
For now, hold this picture.
HiFi reads cluster around fifteen to twenty kilobases.
That length is the sweet spot for spanning genes, transcripts, structural variants, segmental duplications, and most repeats.
The combination of that length with Q30-plus accuracy is what makes HiFi the dominant platform for high-quality genome work in the modern era.
Alright.
We've covered the core technology — zero-mode waveguides, SMRT cells, phospholinked nucleotides, phi29 polymerase, SMRTbells, CCS, HiFi reads, and read length.
That's the first half.
Take ten minutes.
Stretch.
Grab water.
When we come back, we'll move from the technology itself to the platform's commercial history, its modern applications, and how it compares to its competitors.
Back in ten.
